coredata.dc:title: New transparent flexible nanopaper as ultraviolet filter based on red emissive Eu(III) nanofibrillated cellulose
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Zhao
Chang, Hui
Xue, Bailiang
Han, Qing
Lü, Xingqiang
Zhang, Sufeng
Li, Xinping
Zhu, Xunjin
Wong, Wai-kwok
Li, Kecheng
coredata.dc:description: A new kind of highly red emissive and transparent nanopapers as ultraviolet filter are produced from lanthanide complex Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 grafted nanofibrillated cellulose (NFC) by a filtration process using a Buchner funnel. The nanopapers Eu-NFC 1–4 with different thickness (0.023 mm, 1; 0.04 mm, 2; 0.081 mm, 3 and 0.1 mm, 4) possess a fibres with dimensions of approximately 50 nm in diameter and several micrometres in length. Those nanopapers exhibit excellent ultraviolet A (UVA; 320–400 nm) filter property and high optical transmittance (>73% at wavelength of 600 nm). The presence of Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 in Eu-NFC nanopapers can block 97% UVA (at 348 nm) light and convert it into pure red emission (CIE: x = 0.663, y = 0.333) through the efficient triplet-triplet energy transfer process. The efficient red emission can significantly improve the photo-stability of β-diketones type UVA filter. It can sustain for 10 h without decomposition under UV irradiation at 365 nm, which makes it possible to be applied in UVA filters. Moreover, its low coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE: 6.39 ppm K−1 of nanocellulose), is superior to petroleum-based materials for red organic light-emitting devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Transparent nanopapers
β-diketones type UVA filter
Photo-stability
Lanthanide
Pure red emission

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nanopaper Eu-NFC 1–4 and Gd-NFC 4
Preparation and characterization of NFC and Eu-NFC 1–4
UV filter and optical transmittance properties of NFC and Eu-NFC 1–4
Photo-physical properties and energy transfer process of Eu-NFC 1–4

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of NFC (a, c) and Eu-NFC 4 (b, d). Inset was the diameter distribution graphic of Eu-NFC 4.

Optical transmittance of nanopaper NFC and Eu-NFC 1-4, (Insert: the photos of nanopaper Eu-NFC) 4 (a); Optical transmittance of the TTA in MeCN solution at 1 × 105 M at room temperature (b); Optical transmittance of the TTA spread on the nanopaper NFC (c); The regular transparency of Eu-NFC 4 with the UV irradiation time (d), Insert: the transmittance (348 nm) variation with the UV irradiation (356 nm) time.

Visible emission and excitation spectra of nanopaper Eu-NFC 1-4. Inseted are the photos of nanopaper Eu-NFC 4 (flattening or rolling) at luminescent state with an excitation at 365 nm by ultraviolet lamp.

Visible emission spectra of nanopaper Eu-NFC 1-4, excited at 348 nm, the UV irradiation time at 365 nm (a). Inseted are the intensity variation of the 5D0 → 7F2 transition with UV irradiation (365 nm) time.

The mechanism of UV absorption and energy transfer process of Eu3+ for the nanopaper Eu-NFC 1-4; CIE chromaticity graphs with the same color coordinate (x = 0.666, y = 0.331) for the emissions of nanopaper Eu-NFC 1-4. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Stress-strain curves of nanopaper NFC and Eu-NFC 4.
Thermal stability properties of nanopaper NFC and Eu-NFC 4.
The reaction and prepared scheme for the synthesis of NFC, Gd-NFC 4 and Eu-NFC 1–4.
Comparison of the UV filter properties of the approved filters and Eu-NFC 4.

head.ce:title: New transparent flexible nanopaper as ultraviolet filter based on red emissive Eu(III) nanofibrillated cellulose

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shaanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Papermaking Technology and Specialty Paper Development
College of Bioresources Chemical and Materials Engineering
Shaanxi University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new kind of highly red emissive and transparent nanopapers as ultraviolet filter are produced from lanthanide complex Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 grafted nanofibrillated cellulose (NFC) by a filtration process using a Buchner funnel. The nanopapers Eu-NFC 1–4 with different thickness (0.023 mm, 1; 0.04 mm, 2; 0.081 mm, 3 and 0.1 mm, 4) possess a fibres with dimensions of approximately 50 nm in diameter and several micrometres in length. Those nanopapers exhibit excellent ultraviolet A (UVA; 320–400 nm) filter property and high optical transmittance (>73% at wavelength of 600 nm). The presence of Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 in Eu-NFC nanopapers can block 97% UVA (at 348 nm) light and convert it into pure red emission (CIE: x = 0.663, y = 0.333) through the efficient triplet-triplet energy transfer process. The efficient red emission can significantly improve the photo-stability of β-diketones type UVA filter. It can sustain for 10 h without decomposition under UV irradiation at 365 nm, which makes it possible to be applied in UVA filters. Moreover, its low coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE: 6.39 ppm K−1 of nanocellulose), is superior to petroleum-based materials for red organic light-emitting devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Although sun exposure provides many health benefits to humans, such as an improvement in the endogenous prevention of some diseases [1,2], overexposure of ultraviolet (UV) might cause adverse effects, such as skin cancer or cutaneous photo-aging [3]. For this reason, sunscreen with UV blocking property have been developed to prevent the harmful effects of UV radiation [4,5]. According to the wavelength of the light, the UV sunlight at the Earth's surface can be classified as UVA (320–400 nm) and UVB (290–320 nm), and the UVA is approximately 98% [6]. A avobenzone is one of the most popular UVA absorbing molecules used in the cosmetic industry, which belongs to the large family of the β-diketones [7]. However, those of β-diketone type ultraviolet filters can yield potentially toxic photo-adducts when exposed to ultraviolet radiation into reactive species [8]. An added triplet–triplet quenchers (T–T), also termed as excited-state quenchers (ESQ), is attributed to the photo-stability of β-diketone type ultraviolet filters [9]. The common quenchers for UV filters are some organic compounds such as diethylhexyl naphthalate, polysilicone-15 and octocrylene [10,11]. Recently, Chisholm et al. reported that Mo2+ complex of β-diketone is a new class of quenchers. The absorbed high energetic UVA undergoes a rapid internal conversion as 1LLCT→1MLCT and the β-diketone is stable without decomposition up to 25 h [12]. On the other side, the coordination of β-diketone with lanthanide ions can also effectively improve its photo-stability. Carlos tested a series of lanthanide β-diketone complexes under UV irradiation and found there is no severe photo degradation of the β-diketone ligands within 10 h [13]. The absorptive high energetic ultraviolet spectrum of β-diketone may be effectively quenched by lanthanide ions and sensitize the characteristic emission of lanthanide ions, which in turn undergo a radiative emitting process, the so-called lanthanide luminescence sensitization, or antenna effect [14]. Energy quenching efficiency related to the stability of ultraviolet filters is determined by luminescence efficiency of lanthanide ions, which is effected by the energy matching of the triplet state of β-diketone and the first excited state of lanthanide ions [15]. In addition, the incorporation of lanthanide β-diketone complexes with organic polymers can further improve their thermal stability and film-forming property [16–18].

Nanofibrillated cellulose (NFC) as natural organic polymers can be facilely produced from plants, which is an ideal flexible polymer matrices [19]. High speed shearing method, fine grinding, ultrafine comminution and freeze-grind have been used to prepare NFC with a diameter of about 50 nm [20–22]. Functionalized NFC hybrid nanopaper has attracted much interest in the flexible electronics, catalysis, biosensor and zinc-air batteries functions [23–26]. For NFC-based optical materials, low coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE: 12–28 ppm K−1), high tensile strength, thermal stability and recyclability make it easier to be processed in higher temperatures than petroleum-based polymers substrate [27]. Nanopaper based on smaller diameter and higher crystallinity NFC can effectively avoid light scattering and thus resulting in excellent optical transmittance [28]. In addition, NFC was utilized as a polymer matrix of ultraviolet filter materials for its excellent biodegradability, biocompatibility and high-hydrophilic properties [29].

In this paper, a new kind of high red emission, transparent and flexible nanopapers were prepared from lanthanide complex [Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2] grafted NFC using a simple section filtration method. Their ultraviolet filter and photo-physical properties were investigated in detail. The Eu3+ complexation can efficiently transfer the absorbed high energetic UVA into the red emission characteristic of Eu3+ ions, which therefore significantly improve the photo-stability of the conventional UVA filter material of 4, 4, 4-trifluoro-1-(2-thienyl)-1,3-butanedione (TTA).

All chemicals were commercially available and used without further purification. Elemental analyses were performed on a Perkin-Elmer 240C elemental analyzer. Infrared spectra were recorded on a Nicolet Nagna-IR 550 spectrophotometer in the region 4000–400 cm−1 using solid nanopaper NFC and Eu-NFC 1–4. Optical transmittance spectra in the UV–Visible region were recorded on a Cary 300 UV spectrophotometer, and steady-state visible fluorescence, PL excitation spectra on a Photon Technology International (PTI) Alphascan spectrofluorometer and visible decay spectra on a pico-N2 laser system (PTI Time Master). The visible luminescence quantum yield (Ф overall) of the solid state nanopaper was determined by absolute method using an integrating sphere (150 nm diameter, BaSO4 coating) on Edingburgh Instrument FLS928. Three parallel measurements were carried out for each sample, so that the presented Ф overall value corresponds to the arithmetic mean value with the estimated errors of 10%. Surface morphologies were examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-6700F, JEOL, Japan). Transmission electron microscopic (TEM, FEI−TECNAI, G2 S-TWIN) operated at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV was used for NFC and Eu-NFC 4 nanopaper. Leica TCS SP5 confocal laser scanning microscopy (Leica microsystems CMS GmbH, Manheim, Germany) was applied to study the fluorescence of NFC and Eu-NFC 4 nanopaper. The fluorescence imagings were scanned using an averaging mode and constant imaging conditions (laser powers of 500 and 600 V during surface imaging). Stress-strain curves measured on a universal testing machine (CMT6503, Shenzhen SANS Test Machine Co. Ltd., Shenzhen, China). The thermogravimetry (TG) experiments were performed using a DSC200 F3 apparatus (NETZSCH), DTG was performed using a SDT-Q600 apparatus (TA) under a nitrogen atmosphere at a flow rate of 100 mL min−1. The differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) experiments were performed using a DSC200 F3 apparatus (NETZSCH). The heating rate used was 5 °C min−1 from ambient temperature to 600 °C. The thickness of nanopaper was measured three times with a paper thickness meter (PT-4A, China). Inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer Optima 2100DV (PerkinElmer Inc., Shelton, CT, USA), was used as reference method (ICP-OES). Nanopaper Eu-NFC 4 was digested in accordance with further described procedure and the elements was assayed at the wavelengths of 420.50 nm for Eu3+, respectively. The photo-stability of TTA and Eu-NFC 4 was evaluated by monitoring the UV spectra intermittently under continuous exposure to UV irradiation using an 8 W ultraviolet lamp.

According to the method in the literature [30], softwood pulp (1 wt%) was pre-treated by cellulase for 2 h at 50 °C. The slurry was refined using a grinder (MKCA6-2, Masuko Sangyo Co., LTD., Saitama, Japan) at 1500 rpm for 0.5 h. The fibrillation was passed through high-pressure homogenizer at a pressure of 150 MPa and the NFC (1 wt%) was finally obtained after 20 times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nanopaper Eu-NFC 1–4 and Gd-NFC 4
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, Eu-NFC nanopaper was prepared from NFC ethanol suspensions 1 wt% (0.313 g, 0.625 g, 1.25 g and 2.5 g) and Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 (0.2 mg, 0.6 mg, 0.8 mg and 1 mg) in dry EtOH (100 ml). The solution was treated under ultrasonic (400 W, 50 KHz) at room temperature for 5 min. After the film (nanopaper) is made with a filtration process using a Buchner funnel and vacuum drying at 45 °C to constant weight, nanopaper Eu-NFC 1–4 was obtained. The complex Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 (1 mg) was replaced by Gd(TTA)3(H2O)2 (1 mg) to obtain Gd-NFC 4. For comparison, nanopaper NFC was prepared with NFC aqueous suspensions by using the similar filtration method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of NFC and Eu-NFC 1–4
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, NFC was decomposed by cellulase from softwood pulp, and grafted with lanthanide complex Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2, further fabricated into nanopapers Eu-NFC 1–4 with different thickness (0.023 mm, Eu-NFC 1; 0.04 mm, Eu-NFC 2; 0.081 mm, Eu-NFC 3 and 0.1 mm, Eu-NFC 4) via a simple section filtration method (as shown in Scheme 1 ). Eu-NFC 4 was selected for spectroscopic characterizations.

It exhibits characteristic broad peaks at 3302 cm−1 and 2890 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of OH and CH, respectively (Fig. 1S). In addition the absorption peaks at 1585 cm−1, 1524 cm−1 and 615 cm−1 are attributed to the β-diketone ligand (TTA). The Eu3+ ions content (0.57 wt%) was determined by ICP-OES analysis. Supra-pure nitric acid (65%) was used for nanopaper Eu-NFC 4 nitrolysis as well as for the standards preparation in ICP-OES. The distribution of Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 in NFC matrix plays a critical role on the light transmittance, ultraviolet absorption and red light emitting properties of the nanopapers. As expected, the XRD patterns of nanopapers NFC and Eu-NFC 4 present very similar broad peaks at 15° (001), 22° (002), 29° and 41°, which is induced by the typical crystalline structure of NFC (Fig. 2S) [31]. The degree of crystallinity (46%) of Eu-NFC 4 was calculated using the equation: I c = 1-I 1/I 2, where I 1 = 170 is the intensity at the minimum angles 2θ = 18° and I 2 = 316 is the intensity at 2θ = 22° associated with the ordered (crystalline) region of ONFC [32]. Obviously, Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 is well-distributed in the NFC matrix without aggregation and Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 grafted NFC shows almost the same crystal structure as NFC. The morphology of the nanopapers NFC and Eu-NFC 1–4 were studied using scanning electron microscopy and transmission electron microscopic. As shown in Fig. 1 , the NFC and Eu-NFC 1–4 possess fibrous structures with dimensions from 30 nm to 70 nm in diameter and the average size of approximate 50 nm (Inset in Fig. 1). The nanofibres within the nanopapers NFC and Eu-NFC 4 are well distributed and packed densely. The lengths of nanofibres were examined by transmission electron microscopy with values ranged from 1000 nm to 3000 nm, which accords with the morphological characteristics of nanofibers (Fig. 3S) [33]. Definitely, SEM analysis provides proof that Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 is well-distributed in the NFC matrix without aggregation, which makes nanopaper Eu-NFC 4 efficient ultraviolet absorption, luminance and color uniform of composite. Fluorescence imaging through confocal microscopy technique has been widely applied to study the dispersion of optical materials on NFC [34–36]. As shown in Fig. 4S, a red emission was observed for Eu-NFC 4 nanopaper when excited with UV light (405 nm), whereas in the case of the pristine NFC nanopaper, no emission was detected. Although the resolution is enhanced from 10 to 5 μm (Scale bar), the Eu-NFC 4 nanopaper still shows a great color uniform which confirms the Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 is evenly distributed in the NFC matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV filter and optical transmittance properties of NFC and Eu-NFC 1–4
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, pure NFC paper exhibits high crystallinity in densely packing mode, which can effectively avoid the light scattering and make it highly transparent [37]. On the contrast, a regular paper is non-transparent. As shown in Fig. 2 a, Eu-NFC 1–4 exhibit excellent optical transmittance (>73% at wavelength of 600 nm), under which the word of “SUST” could be clearly seen (Inset: 3a), and NFC without Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 exhibits a slightly higher light transmittance (80% at 600 nm). It is also interesting to note that thicker nanopapers led to an enhanced UVA filter property. As expected, the nanopaper Eu-NFC 4 show a filter rate higher than 88% at 270 nm, which should be mainly attributed to π–π* transition of thiophene in β-diketone ligand (TTA), and the π–π* transition of enolic in β-diketone ligand (TTA) dedicates to the exceptionally high UVA filter of 97% at 348 nm was observed [38]. More importantly, Eu-NFC 4 exhibits the highest molar absorption coefficients (≈49370 mol−1cm−1) at UVA region, in comparison to those highly efficient UV filter reported previously (Table 1 ) [39–46]. The photostability of Eu-NFC 4 and the ligand (TTA) were investigated by monitoring their UV–vis spectroscopies after continuous photo-excitation at 365 nm over a period of 10 h. Photostability of a material could be expressed as percent relative absorbance at the maximum wavelength and calculated according to the equation: I t = A t /A o × 100%, where I t is percent relative absorbance after t hour of irradiation, A t is relative absorbance after t hour of irradiation and A o is absorbance before the irradiation. As shown in Fig. 2b–(c), both in solution (CH3CN) and in solid state (spread on nanopaper NFC), the UV absorption of TTA at 330 nm degraded gradually with the increasing UV irradiation time from 0 h to 10 h, and the I t value were decreased from 100 to 11 in solution and 43 in solid. This is mainly because of the enol-to-keto transformation and/or photo-decomposition, which could not be recovered in 10 h after the UV irradiation. In comparison, UV absorption band of Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 (348 nm) in Eu-NFC 4 become blue-shifted by 15 nm and its UV absorption shows a much higher photostability (I t ≈ 100) without obvious change after a continuous UV irradiation at 365 nm over 10 h (Fig. 2d). Without doubt, Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 grafted natural NFC can act as a highly stable and effective ultraviolet filter for sunscreen application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo-physical properties and energy transfer process of Eu-NFC 1–4
ce:section.ce:para:
As shown in Fig. 3 , upon photo excitation at 348 nm (located at UVA), Eu-NFC 1–4 show strong characteristic emissions of Eu3+ ion due to transitions from the emitting level (5D0) to the ground multiple 7FJ (J = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4), with 578 nm for 5D0 → 7F0, 588–595 nm for 5D0 → 7F1, 613 nm for 5D0 → 7F2, 651 nm for 5D0 → 7F3, and 678 nm for 5D0 → 7F4. In addition, a positive relationship between the nanopaper thickness (0.023 mm, Eu-NFC 1; 0.04 mm, Eu-NFC 2; 0.081 mm, Eu-NFC 3 and 0.1 mm, Eu-NFC 4) and red emission intensity at 613 nm was observed (Fig. 4 ). The residual fluorescence emission from the TTA ligand was not detectable in the visible region, which indicate TTA can act as efficient antenna to absorb UV light and transfer to Eu3+, leading to line-like emissions due to 5D0 → 7FJ transitions. The strongest emission (λ
em = 613 nm) based on the hyper-sensitive 5D0 → 7F2 transition, endows the pure red light with the same Commission International De L'Eclairage (CIE) chromatic coordinate x = 0.663 and y = 0.333 for nanopaper Eu-NFC 1–4, as show in Fig. 5 . Besides, the presence of Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 doesn't not affect the flexibility of the NFC nanopaper, which could still be rolled without any cracks at both normal and light-emitting states (inserted photo in Fig. 3). Specifically, the emission spectra of the Eu-NFC 4 (λ max = 613 nm) remain unchanged upon the excitation of 348 nm after the UVA irradiation within 10 h (Fig. 4). Obviously, Eu-NFC 4 is very photo-stable under continuous UVA irradiation (365 nm) over 10 h. To the best of our knowledge, Eu-NFC 4 nanopapers are the first examples based on Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 grafted nanofibrillated cellulose (NFC) and show an excellent photo-stability, which are very promising as active layer for pure red OLEDs applications.

For comparison, the nanopaper Gd-NFC was also prepared, in which the Gd3+ cannot accept any energy from the excited state of the TTA chromorphore because the first excited 6P7/2 state of the Gd3+ ion lies above the triplet state of the chromophore [47]. Gd-NFC shows a typical fluorescence (λ
em = 445 and τ = 1.71 ns) at room temperature, and a phosphorescence (λ
em = 498 and τ = 1.2 ms) at 77 K, from which the triplet (3π-π*) energy level was calculated to be 20080 cm−1. And the singlet (1π-π*) energy level of 25641 cm−1 was estimated from the onset of its UV–visible absorbance at 390 nm. So the energy gap ΔE1 (5561 cm−1, 1π-π*–3π-π*) higher than 5000 cm−1, endows the effective inter-system crossing process according to Reinhouldt's empirical rule [48]. On the other hand, the 3π-π* energy level (20080 cm−1) lies above the first excited state level (17286 cm−1) of Eu3+ ion, and the energy gap (ΔE2 = 2794 nm) for Eu-NFC 1–4 falls into the range of 2500–4000 cm−1 for efficient energy transfer from T1 to Eu3+ ion according to Latva's empirical rule (Fig. 5) [49]. As for nanopaper Eu-NFC 4, the lifetime of emission at 613 nm is measured to be τ obs = 285 μs, which is higher than that of Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 with a value of 190 μs (Fig. 5S) [50]. In addition, the luminescence quantum yield (Ф overall) for nanopaper Eu-NFC 4 was enhanced to 55% in comparison with 38% for pristine Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2. This is because of the replacement of the two coordination water molecules by NFC around the Eu3+ ions in nanopaper Eu-NFC 4, which can diminish the vibrationic quenching and improve the Ф overall accordingly. Except for sensitizing the characteristic red light emission of Eu3+ ions, the non-radiative transition process should present in this system due to the exist of OH- or CH-oscillators in Eu(-NFC nanopaper. Definitely, the nanopaper Eu-NFC 4 as sunscreen material can not only effectively absorb UVA light, but also convert it into much lower energetic red light without increasing the temperature on the surface of the nanopaper.

Nanopapers also show a good processing property and thermal stability. As shown in Fig. 6 , the stress-strain curves indicate that nanopaper NFC possesses a good mechanical property with a tensile strength and of 25.1 MPa and a Young's modulus of 784.5 MPa. The tensile strength and the Young's modulus of nanopaper Eu-NFC 4 slightly decreased to 23.5 MPa and 708.3 MPa, respectively. Moreover, the CTE of nanopaper Eu-NFC 4 is as low as 6.39 ppm/K within the range of 100–242 °C (Fig. 6S), and its initial degradation temperature reaches up to 238.6 °C which was much higher than the practical application temperature. The results of TG-DTG and DSC (Fig. 7 and Figs. 7S and 8S) show both nanopaper NFC and Eu-NFC 4 undergo one step thermal decomposition process, with mass loss rates at about 13.37%/min and 13.58%/min, for NFC in 250–425 °C and Eu-NFC 4 in 240–420 °C, respectively. Obviously, the new composite nanopapers can be practically used within a wide temperature range from 0 to 150 °C.

In this study, nanopaper with excellent UVA blocking properties were successfully prepared from Eu(III) complex grafted NFC by a filtration process using a Buchner funnel. The complex Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 was uniformly distributed in Eu-NFC 1–4 nanopaper and the addition of Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 exhibited only a minor effect on the transparency of the nanopaper in the visible-light region. The Eu-NFC 4 nanopaper can effectively absorb 97% UVA light and convert it into pure red light emission (CIE: x = 0.663, y = 0.333) through the efficient T−T energy transfer process. Furthermore, the efficient energy transfer endow the good photo-stability of Eu-NFC 4 nanopaper, no photobleaching was observed even after UVA (λ = 365 nm) exposure for 10 h and the Eu-NFC 4 nanopaper maintained its UVA blocking and red light emission property. Other than their excellent UVA blocking property, the Eu-NFC 4 nanopaper is also a promising candidate emissive material for OLED because of its high thermal stability and flexibility, and excellent mechanical property with a quite low CTE (6.39 ppm/K).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31370578, 21703131), Doctoral Scientific Research Foundation of Shaanxi University of Science & Technology (2016BJ-40), Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province of China (2014JZ013), Scientific Research Program Foundation of Shaanxi Provincial Education Department (2016JK1102), Innovation Project of Science and Technology of Shaanxi Province (2015KTCQ01-44). X.Z. thanks the supports from Hong Kong Research Grants Council (HKBU 22304115), Hong Kong Baptist University (FRG2/16-17/024, FRG1/15-16/052).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2017.09.039.
